title: The Return of Embodied Pleasure
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: After trauma, pleasure can feel foreign or even dangerous. Learn why your nervous system numbs itself and how to gently reawaken your innate capacity for joy.
imageDescription: Warm, golden light streams into a quiet room, illuminating the delicate, intricate patterns of a kintsugi bowl. The repaired cracks glow, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing. The scene evokes a sense of profound peace and the gentle return of sensation.
htmlContent:
Pleasure is not a reward for healing; it is the pathway. It is the very mechanism through which a nervous system patterned by threat begins to relearn the language of safety, connection, and ease. We have been taught to chase happiness as a destination, a prize to be won after the hard work of recovery is complete, but this fundamentally misunderstands the architecture of our own biology. It uses sensation - the gentle warmth of sunlight on the skin, the satisfying texture of a perfectly ripe piece of fruit, the resonant hum of a cello ~ as the primary data points to recalibrate a world that has felt dangerous for far too long. For those of us who have lived in the grayscale terrain of complex trauma, the return of pleasure is not an indulgence; it is the homecoming we have been quietly, desperately seeking.

The Geography of a Numbed System

To understand why pleasure feels so foreign, even threatening, after long-term trauma, we must first map the territory of a nervous system organized around survival. When an environment is consistently unsafe, the body makes a brilliant and necessary adaptation: it numbs. It dials down sensation, mutes emotional responses, and constricts the capacity for joyful expansion because, in a state of chronic threat, feeling fully is a liability. This is not a psychological failing or a lack of willpower; it is a biological imperative. even good ones - can be dysregulating, and so it opts for a narrow, manageable band of experience. A client once described this to me as living life behind a thick pane of glass; they could see the world happening out there, but the colors were muted, the sounds were muffled, and nothing ever quite touched them. This is the hallmark of the dorsal vagal state, a kind of physiological hibernation where the body conserves energy by shutting down non-essential functions, and in the economy of survival, pleasure is often deemed non-essential.

We spend years, even decades, in this state, and the pathways for pleasure begin to atrophy like unused muscles. The brain, a master of efficiency, prunes the neural circuits for joy and connection because they are not being fired. It strengthens the circuits for threat detection, hypervigilance, and dissociation instead. We become exquisitely attuned to the subtle cues of danger ~ the shift in someone’s tone of voice, the flicker of movement in our peripheral vision - while the cues of safety and delight barely register. The tragedy is not just the presence of pain, but the real absence of its opposite. We forget what it even feels like to inhabit a body that is not a source of alarm but a source of wisdom and wonder. The work back to pleasure, then, is not about adding something new but about reawakening something ancient and inherent that has gone dormant. It is the slow, patient work of reminding the body that the war it has been fighting is, finally, over.

The Tyranny of the Shoulds

The path back to embodied pleasure is often barricaded by a chorus of internal critics and societal expectations... the tyranny of the “shoulds.” We believe we should be further along in our healing, we should be able to feel happy by now, we should be more grateful. This internal pressure creates a paradox: the desperate wanting of pleasure becomes the very thing that pushes it away. It arises from the nervous system’s felt sense of safety, something that cannot be forced or willed into existence. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals try to "achieve" relaxation or "conquer" their anhedonia, only to find themselves more frustrated and disconnected than before. They are trying to apply the logic of the thinking mind to the domain of the feeling body, and it is a fundamental mismatch of operating systems.

For a deeper exploration of these themes, see Kalesh's work bridges ancient and modern healing.

Somatic Permission Slips

Let that land for a moment.

This practice directly challenges the all-or-nothing thinking that trauma so often instills. We do not need to feel 100% safe to begin feeling pleasure. We can feel 5% safe, 10% more at ease, and that is enough. It turns out to be the essence of dual awareness, a cornerstone of many trauma-informed modalities. We are not trying to erase the discomfort, but to expand our capacity to hold it alongside something else. We are widening the window of tolerance, not by blasting it open, but by gently, patiently tapping on the glass until it begins to resonate with a new frequency.

The Language of Sensation

Our culture is deeply impoverished when it comes to the language of sensation. We have a rich and complex vocabulary for our thoughts and emotions, but when it comes to describing the inner terrain of the body, we are often at a loss. Reclaiming embodied pleasure requires that we become poets of our own physiology, learning to notice and name the subtle, ever-shifting currents of sensation that are constantly flowing through us. That becomes not an intellectual exercise; it is a practice of direct, non-judgmental contact with the raw data of our lives.

As we cultivate this sensory literacy, something remarkable begins to happen. We start to discover that our bodies have a preference. They have a "yes" and a "no" that are distinct from the mind's preferences. It is the foundation of setting boundaries that are not just intellectual concepts but lived realities. It is how we move from a life dictated by external pressures and internal critics to a life guided by the deep, quiet wisdom of our own organism.

The Ecology of Pleasure

Pleasure does not arise in a vacuum. It is an emergent property of an entire ecology, a complex interplay between our internal state and our external environment. For too long, we have treated healing as a purely internal process, a matter of fixing our thoughts or regulating our nervous systems in isolation. Our capacity for pleasure is genuinely shaped by the spaces we inhabit, the people we connect with, the food we eat, and the information we consume. A nervous system cannot truly settle into a state of receptive ease if it is constantly being bombarded by stimuli that it perceives as threatening.

In my own process, I have found that one of the most powerful practices is to consciously curate my sensory diet. Just as we choose to eat foods that nourish our bodies, we can choose to consume sights, sounds, and experiences that nourish our nervous systems. It turns out to be an ongoing practice of discernment, of asking, "Does this expand my sense of possibility, or does it contract it? Does this leave me feeling more resourced, or more depleted?" Over time, this practice builds a powerful feedback loop. The more we intentionally seek out and savor these moments of nourishment, the more our nervous system learns to recognize and expect them. The world begins to transform from a place of potential threat to a place of potential resource. We start to live in a fundamentally different reality, not because the world has changed, but because our capacity to perceive its goodness has been restored.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does pleasure feel dangerous or scary after trauma?

After chronic trauma, the nervous system often pairs heightened states of arousal ~ even positive ones like excitement or joy... with danger. The body has learned that big feelings can be a prelude to overwhelm or threat, so it reflexively shuts them down. Allowing pleasure can feel like letting your guard down in a world that has proven to be unsafe, which is why the return to it must be gentle and titrated.

Is it possible to feel pleasure and grief at the same time?

Absolutely. In fact, this capacity for dual awareness is a hallmark of healing. We can feel the deep grief of what was lost while also savoring the simple pleasure of a warm cup of tea. Healing is not about replacing bad feelings with good ones, but about expanding our capacity to hold the full, complex spectrum of human experience with compassion and presence.

Sit with that.

How do I start if I feel completely numb?

Start small, and start with sensation, not emotion. Don't try to feel "happy." Instead, bring your attention to a single, neutral sensation. Notice the feeling of your feet on the floor, the texture of the fabric of your clothes, or the temperature of the air on your skin. The goal is not to force a feeling but to simply practice the art of noticing, which gently reawakens the pathways of sensory awareness.

The Quiet Revolution

The return to embodied pleasure is not a frivolous pursuit; it is a quiet revolution. It is the radical act of reclaiming the body as a safe and worthy home after it has been a site of terror and betrayal. It is a deep political statement in a culture that profits from our disconnection and dissatisfaction. It is the slow, patient, and deeply personal work of rewriting the narrative of a life from one of mere survival to one of genuine, expansive aliveness. It does not happen overnight, and it does not happen in a straight line. It happens in the small, accumulated moments of choosing to notice, to allow, and to receive the goodness that is always, already here, waiting just beneath the surface of our well-rehearsed defenses.

This work asks for a different kind of effort ~ not the striving, muscular effort of the will, but the soft, receptive effort of attention. It asks us to trust that the body has an innate intelligence, a deep and abiding wisdom that knows the way home if we are only willing to listen. It is a path of immense tenderness, a homecoming to the simple, significant truth that we were born with the capacity for pleasure, and that nothing that has happened to us can ever truly extinguish that birthright. It is always there, waiting for us, like a forgotten melody that we can, with patience and with grace, learn to sing again.
